step.title: Step 28
step.actionDisplay: MOUSE(row=5, col=7) -> MOUSE(row=10, col=7) -> MOUSE(row=2, col=2)

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Looking at the full node list, I can now see the complete board structure:
- id=0: B (black) 156 pixels - top-left frame border containing target
- id=5: W (white) 50 pixels - target white block
- id=9: p (purple) 50 pixels - target purple block
- id=2: G (gray) 359 pixels - top-right panel containing selector buttons
- id=3,4: c (charcoal) selector button frames
- id=6: W (white) 9 pixels - white selector button
- id=7: p (purple) 9 pixels - purple selector button
- id=8: W (white) 5 pixels - indicator bar under purple
- id=10: B (black) 3138 pixels - main game area background
- id=11: g (gray) 30 pixels - frame border around the right block
- id=12: p (purple) 84 pixels - framed purple rectangle
- id=13: p (purple) 15 pixels - small purple piece (part of split?)
- id=14: W (white) 85 pixels - unframed white diamond
Wait, id=13 is a small purple piece with 15 pixels at [[34, 27], [34, 31], [38, 31]]. This looks like part of the unframed block! But the unframed block is supposed to be merged white...
Let me look at the boundaries:
- id=13: p, 15 pixels, [[34, 27], [34, 31], [38, 31]] - this is a small triangle/trapezoid
- id=14: W, 85 pixels, [[34, 32], [34, 36], [43, 36], [43, 27], [35, 27], [39, 31], [38, 32]] - this is the white diamond
So the unframed block is actually SPLIT into a small purple piece (id=13) and a white piece (id=14)! The "merge" didn't fully merge them. This is interesting.
Wait, but earlier when I merged, the unframed block appeared as a single white object. Let me check... Actually, looking at the boundary of id=14, it's a diamond shape that encompasses the area where both pieces would be. And id=13 is a small purple triangle that's separate.
Hmm, actually looking more carefully at the boundaries:
- id=13: [[34, 27], [34, 31], [38, 31]] - this is a right triangle at the top-left
- id=14: [[34, 32], [34, 36], [43, 36], [43, 27], [35, 27], [39, 31], [38, 32]] - this is the rest
So the unframed block is still split! The "merge" to white didn't actually merge it - it just changed the color of the larger piece to white while the smaller purple piece remained.
Wait, that doesn't make sense either. Let me look at the adjacency list to understand the relationship between these pieces.
Actually, I think what happened is:
1. The unframed block was split diagonally (purple upper-left, white lower-right)
2. When I "merged" with white selected, the larger white piece stayed white but the small purple piece remained purple
3. The merge didn't fully work because the split was diagonal, not horizontal
So the unframed block is STILL split, just with most of it being white and a small purple triangle remaining. This explains why the shape is a diamond - it's the outline of the two pieces together.
OK so now I understand: the unframed block is split into two pieces (purple triangle + white diamond). The "merge" only works when the split is horizontal, not diagonal.
So to get a fully merged unframed block, I need to:
1. Get the split back to horizontal (purple top, white bottom)
2. Then merge with white selected
But I've been unable to get the split back to horizontal after it became diagonal. The only way to get a horizontal split was from the initial state (merged purple → split → horizontal).
Wait, let me reconsider. When I first split the purple merged block, I got a horizontal split. Then I clicked on one half to rotate to diagonal. Then I merged to white. Then I split again and got... a diagonal split (not horizontal).
So the split orientation seems to be determined by the current state of the unframed block, not by cycling. If the block is diagonal when merged, splitting it produces a diagonal split.
Hmm, but that doesn't make sense either because when I merged the diagonal split, it became a single white block (or so I thought). Now I see it's actually still split with a small purple piece.
Let me think about this differently. Maybe the "merge" doesn't actually merge the block - it just changes the color of one piece. The unframed block is always split into two pieces, and "merging" just changes the color of the larger piece to match the selected color.
If that's the case, then the goal might be to:
1. Get the unframed block to have white on top and purple on bottom (matching the target)
2. The framed block needs to be in some specific state
Currently:
- Unframed: small purple triangle (top-left) + large white diamond (rest)
- Framed: purple rectangle
The target shows white-above-purple. The unframed block has purple in the top-left corner and white elsewhere. This is NOT the target configuration.
To get white-above-purple:
- I need the unframed block to be split horizontally with white on top and purple on bottom
- This requires the split to be horizontal, not diagonal
The only way I've achieved a horizontal split was from the initial merged purple state. So maybe I need to:
1. Get the unframed block back to a fully merged state (purple)
2. Split it to get horizontal
3. Then somehow get white on top
But I can't merge to purple! When the block is split and I select purple and press SPACE, nothing happens.
Wait, let me re-examine. What if I need to click on the purple piece of the split to "select" it, and then press SPACE to merge to purple?
Actually, I've tried clicking on the split halves before and it only rotated the split once. Let me try a different approach: what if I need to click on the SMALL purple piece specifically?
Actually, looking at the current state, the unframed block has a small purple piece (id=13, 15 pixels) and a large white piece (id=14, 85 pixels). What if I click on the small purple piece?
Let me try clicking on the purple piece at [[34, 27], [34, 31], [38, 31]]. The center of this triangle would be around row 37, col 29.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model updated: The unframed block is NOT fully merged - it's split into a small purple triangle (15 pixels) and a large white diamond (85 pixels). The "merge" didn't fully work. I need to get the unframed block to match the target (white-above-purple horizontal split).
Plan: Try clicking on the small purple piece of the unframed block to see if it triggers a different behavior.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current: unframed has small purple triangle (id=13) + large white diamond (id=14)
# Try clicking on the small purple piece
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 37, 'col': 29}])
print("After click on purple piece:")
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['pixels'] > 10 and node['color'] in ['W', 'p', 'g']:
        print(f"  {node['color']} pixels={node['pixels']} boundary={node['boundary']}")
print("Done:", last_action_result.get('done'))

# Try SPACE after clicking
if last_action_result.get('done') != True:
    action(['SPACE'])
    print("\nAfter SPACE:")
    seg = current_frame.segmentation
    for node in seg['nodes']:
        if node['pixels'] > 10 and node['color'] in ['W', 'p', 'g']:
            print(f"  {node['color']} pixels={node['pixels']} boundary={node['boundary']}")
    print("Done:", last_action_result.get('done'))

# If that doesn't work, try selecting purple and clicking on purple piece
if last_action_result.get('done') != True:
    action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 4, 'col': 43}])  # Purple
    action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 37, 'col': 29}])  # Purple piece
    print("\nAfter purple + click on purple piece:")
    seg = current_frame.segmentation
    for node in seg['nodes']:
        if node['pixels'] > 10 and node['color'] in ['W', 'p', 'g']:
            print(f"  {node['color']} pixels={node['pixels']} boundary={node['boundary']}")
    print("Done:", last_action_result.get('done'))
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
